concept.headline: data class auto-generates equals, hashCode, toString, and copy — turning boilerplate model objects into a one-liner.
concept.explanation: Adding the data modifier to a class tells Kotlin to generate equals() (compares all constructor properties), hashCode() (consistent with equals), toString() (readable format), and copy() (clone with optional overrides). Destructuring declarations let you unpack a data class into multiple variables: val (x, y) = point.
concept.syntax:
data class Point(val x: Int, val y: Int)

val p1 = Point(1, 2)
println(p1)            // Point(x=1, y=2)

val p2 = p1.copy(y = 5)
println(p2)            // Point(x=1, y=5)

println(p1 == Point(1, 2))  // true — structural equality

// Destructuring
val (x, y) = p2
println("x=$x y=$y")   // x=1 y=5
keyPoints: data class auto-generates: toString, equals, hashCode, copy
== on data classes compares content, not reference

demo.intro: Watch a data class instance being printed, copied with a change, and destructured.
demo.code:
data class Point(val x: Int, val y: Int)

fun main() {
    val p = Point(1, 2)
    println(p)
    val p2 = p.copy(y = 5)
    println(p2)
    val (x, y) = p2
    println("x=$x y=$y")
}

explanation.what: val p = Point(1, 2) — create a data class instance
explanation.intent: Show data class construction — identical to a regular class
explanation.misconception: data class is still a regular class — it just has auto-generated methods. You can add your own methods and the generated ones don't interfere.

explanation.why: data class generates toString() as ClassName(prop=value, ...) automatically
explanation.intent: Show auto-generated toString — no manual implementation needed
explanation.misconception: A regular (non-data) class would print something like 'Point@3764951d' — the memory address. data class generates a human-readable representation.

explanation.what: p.copy(y = 5) — creates a new Point with x kept from p and y changed to 5
explanation.why: copy() clones all properties and lets you override specific ones by name
explanation.intent: Show copy with selective override — the immutable update pattern
explanation.misconception: p is not modified — copy() returns a NEW object. p still equals Point(x=1, y=2). This is the idiomatic way to 'update' an immutable data object.

explanation.why: p2 has x=1 (copied from p) and y=5 (overridden)
explanation.intent: Confirm copy produced a new object with the override applied
explanation.misconception: p2 is a completely separate object from p — modifying a var property of p2 (if it had any) would not affect p

explanation.why: data class generates componentN() functions; val (x, y) = ... calls component1() and component2()
explanation.change: x = 1, y = 5 as separate local variables
explanation.intent: Show destructuring as a concise way to extract properties
explanation.misconception: Destructuring follows position order of constructor parameters — (x, y) maps to x first, y second. If you swap them you get the wrong values.

explanation.what: println("x=$x y=$y") uses the destructured local variables
explanation.why: x = 1, y = 5 from the destructuring above
explanation.intent: Show destructured variables used in a string template
explanation.misconception: x and y here are new local variables, not references to p2.x and p2.y — they are copies of the values

prediction.question:
What does this print?

data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
val u1 = User("Alice", 30)
val u2 = User("Alice", 30)
println(u1 == u2)
options: true — data class compares by content
prediction.explanation: data class generates equals() that compares all constructor properties. u1 and u2 have identical name and age, so u1 == u2 is true — even though they are different objects in memory.

explanation.afterDemo: data class is one of Kotlin's most-used features. It replaces the Java ceremony of manually writing equals/hashCode/toString for model objects. The copy() function enables the immutable update pattern — create a modified clone instead of mutating the original. Destructuring makes extracting values from data objects clean and readable.
keyTakeaways: data class generates toString, equals, hashCode, copy — no boilerplate.
== compares content (structural equality), not reference identity.
copy(prop = newValue) creates a modified clone — the safe way to 'update' immutable data.

practice.prompt: Create a data class Config(val host: String, val port: Int). Make an instance Config("localhost", 8080), copy it changing port to 9090, and print both.
practice.solutionCode:
data class Config(val host: String, val port: Int)

fun main() {
    val c1 = Config("localhost", 8080)
    val c2 = c1.copy(port = 9090)
    println(c1)
    println(c2)
}
practice.hint: Use c1.copy(port = 9090) to create c2. println will use the auto-generated toString.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! copy() preserved host and changed only port. toString() formatted both automatically.
retryHints: c1.copy(port = 9090) — named argument overrides only port
println uses auto-generated toString: Config(host=..., port=...)
Expected: Config(host=localhost, port=8080) then Config(host=localhost, port=9090)

summary.title: Data Classes — Complete
takeaways: data class auto-generates toString, equals, hashCode, copy — use it for model/value objects.
copy(prop = val) is the idiomatic immutable update — returns a new instance.
Destructuring: val (a, b) = dataObj unpacks by constructor order.
